title: Anastomotic Leak
oneLiner: A surgical anastomosis fails, spilling luminal contents into the peritoneal cavity – a life-threatening emergency most common after colorectal surgery.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**

You are bleeped to the surgical ward at 03:00. Mrs. K, a 68-year-old woman, is on day 6 post anterior resection for rectal cancer. The nursing staff report she is “not quite right” – her heart rate is 110 bpm, she has a temperature of 38.0°C, and she’s complaining of vague, diffuse abdominal discomfort. She had passed flatus yesterday but not today. On examination, she is mildly tender around her midline wound, but not peritonitic. Her wound appears clean and dry. Her urine output over the last 4 hours is only 80 ml.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues and their meaning:**

- **Post-operative day 5-7:** This is the classic time window because collagen lysis (from wound healing) peaks around day 5, before new collagen synthesis begins. The anastomosis is weakest then.
- **Tachycardia (110 bpm):** The earliest sign of hypovolaemia or sepsis – often present *before* fever or hypotension. Do not dismiss as “pain” or “anxiety”.
- **Low-grade fever (38.0°C):** Systemic inflammatory response triggered by bacterial translocation through the leaking suture line.
- **Vague, diffuse abdominal discomfort:** Early leak causes chemical peritonitis (from bile/pancreatic enzymes) that is initially non-specific. Pain becomes more localised as bacterial peritonitis develops.
- **“Not quite right” – nursing gestalt:** Often the most sensitive clue – patients look “off” or “washed out”. Trust your nurses.
- **Reduced urine output (oliguria):** Third-spacing of fluid into the peritoneal cavity due to inflammation, leading to intravascular depletion.
- **Passed flatus yesterday but not today:** Transient ileus from the leak has now caused functional obstruction. A patient who was improving may suddenly deteriorate.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **The “painless” leak:** Elderly or immunocompromised patients may have minimal pain and only present with confusion, tachycardia, and a rising lactate. Missed because no one palpates the abdomen properly.
- **The shoulder tip / chest pain mimic:** A high leak (e.g., oesophagojejunostomy) can irritate the diaphragm, referred to the shoulder or as pleuritic chest pain. ECG and CXR may be normal – if you stop there, you miss the leak.
- **The wound discharge variant:** A small, contained leak may track along a drain or through the wound, presenting as a serous or feculent discharge from the incision. Patients often look surprisingly well initially. Don’t assume it’s a simple wound infection – probe the wound… cautiously.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**

An anastomotic leak is a breach in the surgical connection (anastomosis) between two segments of bowel (or other hollow organ). Intraluminal contents – bacteria, digestive enzymes, faecal material – spill into the normally sterile peritoneal cavity or surrounding tissues, triggering a chemical and then bacterial peritonitis.

**Why this topic matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Occurs in 2-8% of colorectal anastomoses, but up to 15-20% in low rectal or oesophageal resections. It is the most feared early post-operative complication in gastrointestinal surgery.
- **Stakes:** Mortality ranges from 6-30%. Survivors often suffer long-term consequences: permanent stoma, chronic sepsis, fistulae, and poor oncological outcomes (leak-associated recurrence).
- **Common pitfalls:** The early signs are subtle and non-specific. Junior doctors often attribute tachycardia to pain or dehydration, order a routine blood panel, and wait for cultures – by then the patient may be in septic shock. Pattern recognition trumps test-ordering here.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of how it develops:**

Imagine the anastomosis as a healing wound between two pieces of bowel. Immediately after surgery, the wound is held together only by sutures/staples (mechanical strength). Over the next 3-5 days, inflammatory cells infiltrate and begin breaking down old collagen (collagenolysis) to make way for new tissue. Around day 5-7, collagenolytic activity peaks, while new collagen synthesis has only just begun – the anastomosis is at its weakest point. Any factor that impairs healing (ischaemia, infection, tension, poor nutrition) can cause a microscopic or macroscopic defect to open.

Once the seal breaks, luminal contents leak. Bile and pancreatic enzymes cause intense chemical irritation (chemical peritonitis). Bacteria (E. coli, Bacteroides, enterococci) then proliferate, causing bacterial peritonitis. The peritoneum reacts with vasodilation, increased capillary permeability, and fluid exudation (third-spacing). This leads to hypovolaemia, reduced cardiac output, and ultimately septic shock if untreated.

**Why each key clinical sign happens – mechanism → sign:**

- **Tachycardia:** Compensatory response to hypovolaemia (third-space loss) + early cytokine-mediated vasodilation. Happens before fever.
- **Fever:** Pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α) released from peritoneal inflammation reset the hypothalamic thermostat.
- **Abdominal pain:** Peritoneal irritation initially from chemicals, then bacteria. Pain may be diffuse or localised depending on the leak location and the patient's ability to wall it off.
- **Oliguria:** Hypovolaemia due to third-spacing. Kidneys retain fluid in an attempt to preserve circulating volume. Lactate may also rise due to tissue hypoperfusion.
- **Wound discharge / drain output:** If the leak is contained or tracks along a drain, the fluid may be turbid, bilious, faeculent, or simply serous with elevated amylase (if near pancreas).

**Connecting pathophysiology to pattern recognition cues:**

The classic day 5-7 timing is not random – it is the nadir of anastomotic strength. The tachycardia and “not quite right” feeling precede the fever because the inflammatory cascade takes hours to raise core temperature. The vague pain may be interpreted as simple ileus unless you specifically think “leak” and examine for rebound or guarding (early). The oliguria is a red flag that hypovolaemia is already significant.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Specific Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Technical (most common overall)** | Poor blood supply (ischaemia) – from aggressive devascularisation, tension on the anastomosis, or haematoma | “Surgeon noted the bowel ends looked dusky before completing the anastomosis” or “Patient had prior radiotherapy” |
| | Inadequate suturing / stapling – full-thickness bites not taken, stapler misfire | “Surgeon was struggling with a narrow pelvis” or “Converted from laparoscopic to open due to bleeding” |
| | Distal obstruction – stricture, faecal impaction, or twisted loop downstream | “Patient had a previous Hartmann’s – reversal was delayed” or “Post-op ileus lasting >5 days” |
| **Patient-related** | Malnutrition – albumin <30 g/L, recent weight loss >10% | “Pre-op albumin 28, NRS-2002 score >3” |
| | Immunosuppression – corticosteroids, chemotherapy, biologics | “On prednisolone 20 mg for IBD” |
| | Smoking – impairs microvascular perfusion | “Current smoker, 30 pack-years” |
| | Diabetes (uncontrolled) – poor wound healing, microangiopathy | “HbA1c 78 mmol/mol” |
| | Obesity – increased tension, poor vascularity | “BMI 38” |
| **Local factors** | Intra-abdominal infection at time of surgery – diverticulitis, abscess | “Urgent surgery for perforated diverticulitis” |
| | Radiotherapy (pelvic) – ischemic microvasculopathy | “Received neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy for low rectal cancer” |
| **High-risk procedures** | Low rectal anastomosis (<5 cm from anal verge) | “Ultra-low anterior resection” |
| | Oesophagogastric anastomosis | “Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy” |
| | Emergency surgery (contaminated field) | “Laparotomy for perforated viscus” |

**Highlight:** The most common cause is **ischaemia** – often multifactorial (technical + patient factors). Never assume a single cause; think of the anastomotic “triangle” – perfusion, tension, and healing environment.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**

There is no universal diagnostic score for anastomotic leak, but the **Dutch Leak Score** (for colorectal leak) uses four variables: fever >38°C (2 points), tachycardia >100 bpm (2), CRP >140 mg/L on day 4-5 (2), and clinical suspicion (1). A score ≥4 has 90% sensitivity for leak. Not mandatory, but useful for threshold thinking.

**Targeted investigations:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Full blood count** | White cell count (WCC), neutrophils | Marker of infection/inflammation | WCC >12 or <4 (left shift), rising trend. But up to 30% of leaks have normal WCC initially. |
| **CRP** | Acute phase reactant | Most useful serial biomarker | CRP >140 mg/L on post-op day 4-5 is a strong predictor. Rising CRP after initial drop is a leak until proven otherwise. |
| **Lactate** | Tissue hypoperfusion / anaerobic metabolism | Identify shock early | Lactate >2 mmol/L in a post-op patient is alarming. >4 is critical. Interpret in context of fluid status and pressors. |
| **Arterial blood gas** | pH, base deficit, lactate | Assess metabolic acidosis | Base deficit < -4 or lactate >2 suggests significant tissue ischaemia or infection. |
| **CT with IV + oral contrast** | Perianastomotic fluid, extraluminal air, contrast extravasation | Gold standard imaging | **Extravasation of oral contrast into the peritoneal cavity is diagnostic.** Free air *may* be normal within first 5 days (surgical emphysema), but a new collection of gas/fluid juxtaposed to the anastomosis is highly suspicious. |
| **CT without contrast** | Usually not sufficient | Only if contraindication to IV contrast or suspected free perforation | Can show collections but misses subtle leaks. |
| **Erect chest X-ray** | Free air under diaphragm | Quick bedside test for perforation | Free air may be present post-laparotomy for up to 7 days. New or increasing free air with clinical deterioration = leak. |
| **Drain fluid analysis** | Amylase, bilirubin, culture | If radiological drain in situ | Amylase >500 U/L in an upper GI leak; bilirubin in biliary leak; culture guides antibiotics. |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms / rules out?**

- **Confirms:** CT with oral contrast showing extravasation outside the bowel lumen.
- **Rules out (near 100% negative predictive value):** A technically adequate CT with IV + oral contrast that shows no extraluminal contrast, no perianastomotic collection, and no free air *in a clinically stable patient*. However, if the patient is unstable, negative imaging should not delay surgical exploration.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – resuscitation and recognition:**

- **Stop oral intake (NBM):** Prevent further spillage. Remove nasogastric tube if in situ (for decompression).
- **IV fluids:** Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline – give a 500 ml bolus stat, then reassess. Aim for urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hour.
- **Blood cultures and start broad-spectrum antibiotics:** Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV (given over 30 min) every 8 hours. If penicillin-allergic, meropenem 1 g IV every 8 hours. Cover Gram-negatives and anaerobes.
- **Call surgical registrar / consultant immediately:** This is not a “review in 30 minutes”. The decision to operate or drain needs senior input now.
- **Consider vasopressors if hypotensive despite fluid:** Noradrenaline (norepinephrine) IV infusion, start 0.05 mcg/kg/min and titrate to MAP >65 mmHg. Do not delay definitive source control for fluid resuscitation.
- **Imaging:** If patient stable enough for CT, go immediately. If unstable, go directly to theatre.

**Common pitfall (first hour):** Do NOT give oral contrast for CT if you suspect a leak – it can be given, but many radiologists prefer water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) which is safer if extravasated. But if the patient is peritonitic, don’t wait for CT – go straight to laparotomy.

**Ward (24-72 hours) – source control and support:**

- **Surgical reintervention:** Either laparoscopic or open. Options: repair the leak (rarely possible), resect and re-anastomose (if healthy tissue), or most commonly: **de-function** – take down the anastomosis, create an end stoma (colostomy/ileostomy), and wash out the abdomen.
- **If contained leak with drainable collection:** CT-guided percutaneous drainage may be attempted if the patient is stable, no generalised peritonitis, and the collection is well-defined. Leave a drain.
- **Continue IV antibiotics:** Duration depends on source control – usually 5-7 days if controlled, longer if ongoing sepsis.
- **Nutritional support:** NBM for at least 5-7 days. TPN (total parenteral nutrition) if prolonged NBM (>7 days) or if the patient is malnourished. Start early (within 48 hours) if pre-existing malnutrition.
- **Monitor:** Daily CRP, WCC, lactate, urine output, fluid balance. Trending CRP downward is a good sign; rising or plateauing suggests ongoing infection.

**Common pitfall (ward phase):** Do NOT restart oral feeds “to see if the leak seals itself” – this will worsen contamination. Do NOT use oral antibiotics in a patient with a leak – they won’t reach the peritoneal cavity effectively.

**Long-term – reconstruction and follow-up:**

- **If a stoma was formed:** Plan for reversal in 3-6 months after resolution of sepsis, adequate nutrition, and treatment of any underlying cause (e.g., smoking cessation, diabetes control). Repeat imaging (contrast enema or colonoscopy) to confirm the defunctioned segment has healed.
- **Enterocutaneous fistula:** May develop if the leak tracks to the skin. Manage with NBM, TPN, octreotide (50-100 mcg SC TID) to reduce GI secretions, and negative pressure wound therapy. Surgical closure after 6-12 months when inflammation has resolved.
- **Oncological implications:** Anastomotic leak after cancer surgery is associated with higher local recurrence risk. These patients need closer surveillance with CEA and colonoscopy.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Anastomotic Leak | Pancreatitis (post-op) | Wound Infection | Ileus (benign) |
|---------|-----------------|------------------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Onset** | Day 5-7 | Day 1-3 | Day 4-7 | Day 1-4 |
| **Pain** | Diffuse, then localised to peritoneum | Epigastric, radiating to back | Localised to wound site | Crampy, poorly localised |
| **Exam** | Guarding, rebound, wound may be clean | Epigastric tenderness, sometimes Cullen’s/Grey-Turner’s | Erythema, warmth, purulent discharge | Mild distension, no peritonism |
| **Vital signs** | Tachycardia, fever, later hypotension | Tachycardia, fever, hypotension can occur | Low-grade fever, normal HR | Usually normal (mild tachycardia if hypovolaemic) |
| **Labs** | Rising CRP, WCC, lactate, base deficit | Elevated amylase/lipase (>3x normal), CRP high | WCC high, CRP moderate, lactate normal | Normal or mildly elevated WCC |
| **Imaging** | CT: extraluminal contrast, air/fluid collection near anastomosis | CT: pancreatic oedema, necrosis, peripancreatic stranding | US/CT: subcutaneous collection, no intra-abdominal fluid | CT: dilated bowel loops, no free fluid, no collection |
| **Key differentiator** | Oral contrast extravasation on CT | Amylase/lipase >3x normal | Wound purulence, normal peritoneum | Clinical improvement with bowel rest and fluids |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Generalised peritonitis** | Severe abdominal pain, board-like rigidity, hypotension, fever | Absent bowel sounds, guarding, rebound, septic shock | Emergency laparotomy, source control, IV antibiotics, ICU admission |
| **Intra-abdominal abscess** | Persistent fever, vague pain, rising CRP/neutrophilia, no bowel function | May have localised tenderness on US/CT | CT-guided percutaneous drainage (if safe) or surgical drainage. Culture-directed antibiotics. |
| **Enterocutaneous fistula** | Drain or wound discharges feculent fluid; patient may develop skin excoriation | High-output fistula >500 ml/day leads to dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, malnutrition | NBM, TPN, octreotide, skin protection (stoma bag), delayed surgical closure (6-12 months) |
| **Septic shock / multi-organ failure** | Hypotension despite fluids, rising lactate, reduced GCS, rising creatinine, need for vasopressors | Lactate >4, base deficit < -8, oliguria, acute kidney injury, respiratory failure | ICU admission, central line, invasive monitoring, vasopressors (noradrenaline), definitive source control. Mortality >30%. |
| **Late presentation – chronic leak / microleak** | Persistent low-grade inflammation, poor wound healing, recurrent ileus, eventual abscess | CRP never normalises, patient fails to progress, may have intermittent fevers | CT with contrast, consider gastrografin enema (for colorectal) to identify occult leak. May require elective repair. |
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic – “LEAKs” for early suspicion:**

- **L** – Look at the **lactate** (any rise >2 is suspicious)
- **E** – **Eyes** – does the patient look unwell (“not right”)?
- **A** – **Abdominal exam** – do not skip a thorough palpation for guarding
- **K** – **KUB** (or CT) – get imaging early
- **S** – **Sepsis screen** – blood cultures, CRP, WCC, lactate

**Mnemonic – “THINK LEAK” for triggers:**

- **T** – Tachycardia (unexplained)
- **H** – Hypotension (new or worsening)
- **I** – Ileus (persistent >5 days)
- **N** – Nutrition (poor pre-op, low albumin)
- **K** – Kind of surgery (low anterior resection, oesophageal)
- **L** – Leucocytosis / raised CRP
- **E** – Electrolyte disturbances
- **A** – Acidosis (metabolic)
- **K** – Keep an open mind – don’t attribute everything to “normal post-op course”

**5-7 high-yield clinical pearls (pattern recognition cues):**

1. **Day 5-7 is the danger zone.** Any unexplained tachycardia, even without fever, on day 4-6 after GI surgery is an anastomotic leak until proven otherwise.
2. **CRP >140 mg/L on day 4 is more sensitive than any clinical sign alone.** Use it as your “rule-out” test – if CRP <120 on day 4, leak is very unlikely.
3. **Pain is not always present.** Elderly, diabetic, or steroid-dependent patients may have silent leaks – look for confusion, oliguria, and rising lactate.
4. **The wound does not have to look infected.** Many leaks occur with a clean, dry wound. Do not be falsely reassured.
5. **Lactate >2 is a red flag** – don’t dismiss as “poor venous access” or “tourniquet”. Repeat arterial gas stat.
6. **Never give oral antibiotics to a patient you suspect of having a leak.** They will not reach the peritoneal cavity and may worsen the contamination.
7. **If you are unsure whether to call the consultant, call.** It is better to be wrong about a leak than to be late.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Attributing tachycardia to pain (give analgesia, recheck – if still tachy, think leak).
- Ordering a “routine” set of bloods and waiting for results before calling a senior.
- Forgetting to assess urine output – it’s the only objective measure of volume status.
- Misinterpreting free air on CT as post-operative – any new or increasing free air beyond day 4 is pathological.
- Starting oral feeds “to stimulate the bowel” – can turn a contained leak into a diffuse peritonitis.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call for help – thresholds to act:**

- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (especially with respiratory rate >20, oxygen saturation <94%, or systolic BP <100 mmHg) – call surgical registrar immediately.
- **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** on any arterial sample – escalate, do not wait for repeat.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** – this is a **critical** finding – call consultant and consider ICU.
- **GCS < 15 or acute confusion** – possible sepsis-related encephalopathy – call ICU outreach.
- **Peritonitis on examination** (guarding, rigidity, rebound) – immediate surgical review; patient likely needs theatre.
- **Sustained hypotension** (SBP <90 mmHg after 500 ml fluid bolus) – start noradrenaline and call consultant/anaesthetist.
- **Urine output <0.5 ml/kg/hour for >2 hours** – despite fluid challenges – sign of ongoing hypovolaemia or developing acute kidney injury – escalate.
- **Any concern from nursing staff** that the patient looks “unwell” – trust it. Get a senior to review.

**When to call the crash team / ICU:**

- If the patient has a **cardiac or respiratory arrest** – obviously.
- If **SBP <80 mmHg despite 2 L fluid and vasopressors started** – call ICU for central line admission.
- If **GCS deteriorates to <8** – protect airway, call anaesthetics.
- If **lactate >6 mmol/L** despite initial resuscitation – likely need organ support.

**Quote to remember:** “When you think of an anastomotic leak, you are already late. You need to have thought of it yesterday.” Act early, act decisively. Your patient’s survival depends on it.

## Anastomotic Leak

**One-liner:** A surgical anastomosis fails, spilling luminal contents into the peritoneal cavity – a life-threatening emergency most common after colorectal surgery.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**

You are bleeped to the surgical ward at 03:00. Mrs. K, a 68-year-old woman, is on day 6 post anterior resection for rectal cancer. The nursing staff report she is “not quite right” – her heart rate is 110 bpm, she has a temperature of 38.0°C, and she’s complaining of vague, diffuse abdominal discomfort. She had passed flatus yesterday but not today. On examination, she is mildly tender around her midline wound, but not peritonitic. Her wound appears clean and dry. Her urine output over the last 4 hours is only 80 ml.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues and their meaning:**

- **Post-operative day 5-7:** This is the classic time window because collagen lysis (from wound healing) peaks around day 5, before new collagen synthesis begins. The anastomosis is weakest then.
- **Tachycardia (110 bpm):** The earliest sign of hypovolaemia or sepsis – often present *before* fever or hypotension. Do not dismiss as “pain” or “anxiety”.
- **Low-grade fever (38.0°C):** Systemic inflammatory response triggered by bacterial translocation through the leaking suture line.
- **Vague, diffuse abdominal discomfort:** Early leak causes chemical peritonitis (from bile/pancreatic enzymes) that is initially non-specific. Pain becomes more localised as bacterial peritonitis develops.
- **“Not quite right” – nursing gestalt:** Often the most sensitive clue – patients look “off” or “washed out”. Trust your nurses.
- **Reduced urine output (oliguria):** Third-spacing of fluid into the peritoneal cavity due to inflammation, leading to intravascular depletion.
- **Passed flatus yesterday but not today:** Transient ileus from the leak has now caused functional obstruction. A patient who was improving may suddenly deteriorate.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **The “painless” leak:** Elderly or immunocompromised patients may have minimal pain and only present with confusion, tachycardia, and a rising lactate. Missed because no one palpates the abdomen properly.
- **The shoulder tip / chest pain mimic:** A high leak (e.g., oesophagojejunostomy) can irritate the diaphragm, referred to the shoulder or as pleuritic chest pain. ECG and CXR may be normal – if you stop there, you miss the leak.
- **The wound discharge variant:** A small, contained leak may track along a drain or through the wound, presenting as a serous or feculent discharge from the incision. Patients often look surprisingly well initially. Don’t assume it’s a simple wound infection – probe the wound… cautiously.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**

An anastomotic leak is a breach in the surgical connection (anastomosis) between two segments of bowel (or other hollow organ). Intraluminal contents – bacteria, digestive enzymes, faecal material – spill into the normally sterile peritoneal cavity or surrounding tissues, triggering a chemical and then bacterial peritonitis.

**Why this topic matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Occurs in 2-8% of colorectal anastomoses, but up to 15-20% in low rectal or oesophageal resections. It is the most feared early post-operative complication in gastrointestinal surgery.
- **Stakes:** Mortality ranges from 6-30%. Survivors often suffer long-term consequences: permanent stoma, chronic sepsis, fistulae, and poor oncological outcomes (leak-associated recurrence).
- **Common pitfalls:** The early signs are subtle and non-specific. Junior doctors often attribute tachycardia to pain or dehydration, order a routine blood panel, and wait for cultures – by then the patient may be in septic shock. Pattern recognition trumps test-ordering here.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story of how it develops:**

Imagine the anastomosis as a healing wound between two pieces of bowel. Immediately after surgery, the wound is held together only by sutures/staples (mechanical strength). Over the next 3-5 days, inflammatory cells infiltrate and begin breaking down old collagen (collagenolysis) to make way for new tissue. Around day 5-7, collagenolytic activity peaks, while new collagen synthesis has only just begun – the anastomosis is at its weakest point. Any factor that impairs healing (ischaemia, infection, tension, poor nutrition) can cause a microscopic or macroscopic defect to open.

Once the seal breaks, luminal contents leak. Bile and pancreatic enzymes cause intense chemical irritation (chemical peritonitis). Bacteria (E. coli, Bacteroides, enterococci) then proliferate, causing bacterial peritonitis. The peritoneum reacts with vasodilation, increased capillary permeability, and fluid exudation (third-spacing). This leads to hypovolaemia, reduced cardiac output, and ultimately septic shock if untreated.

**Why each key clinical sign happens – mechanism → sign:**

- **Tachycardia:** Compensatory response to hypovolaemia (third-space loss) + early cytokine-mediated vasodilation. Happens before fever.
- **Fever:** Pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α) released from peritoneal inflammation reset the hypothalamic thermostat.
- **Abdominal pain:** Peritoneal irritation initially from chemicals, then bacteria. Pain may be diffuse or localised depending on the leak location and the patient's ability to wall it off.
- **Oliguria:** Hypovolaemia due to third-spacing. Kidneys retain fluid in an attempt to preserve circulating volume. Lactate may also rise due to tissue hypoperfusion.
- **Wound discharge / drain output:** If the leak is contained or tracks along a drain, the fluid may be turbid, bilious, faeculent, or simply serous with elevated amylase (if near pancreas).

**Connecting pathophysiology to pattern recognition cues:**

The classic day 5-7 timing is not random – it is the nadir of anastomotic strength. The tachycardia and “not quite right” feeling precede the fever because the inflammatory cascade takes hours to raise core temperature. The vague pain may be interpreted as simple ileus unless you specifically think “leak” and examine for rebound or guarding (early). The oliguria is a red flag that hypovolaemia is already significant.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Specific Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Technical (most common overall)** | Poor blood supply (ischaemia) – from aggressive devascularisation, tension on the anastomosis, or haematoma | “Surgeon noted the bowel ends looked dusky before completing the anastomosis” or “Patient had prior radiotherapy” |
| | Inadequate suturing / stapling – full-thickness bites not taken, stapler misfire | “Surgeon was struggling with a narrow pelvis” or “Converted from laparoscopic to open due to bleeding” |
| | Distal obstruction – stricture, faecal impaction, or twisted loop downstream | “Patient had a previous Hartmann’s – reversal was delayed” or “Post-op ileus lasting >5 days” |
| **Patient-related** | Malnutrition – albumin <30 g/L, recent weight loss >10% | “Pre-op albumin 28, NRS-2002 score >3” |
| | Immunosuppression – corticosteroids, chemotherapy, biologics | “On prednisolone 20 mg for IBD” |
| | Smoking – impairs microvascular perfusion | “Current smoker, 30 pack-years” |
| | Diabetes (uncontrolled) – poor wound healing, microangiopathy | “HbA1c 78 mmol/mol” |
| | Obesity – increased tension, poor vascularity | “BMI 38” |
| **Local factors** | Intra-abdominal infection at time of surgery – diverticulitis, abscess | “Urgent surgery for perforated diverticulitis” |
| | Radiotherapy (pelvic) – ischemic microvasculopathy | “Received neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy for low rectal cancer” |
| **High-risk procedures** | Low rectal anastomosis (<5 cm from anal verge) | “Ultra-low anterior resection” |
| | Oesophagogastric anastomosis | “Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy” |
| | Emergency surgery (contaminated field) | “Laparotomy for perforated viscus” |

**Highlight:** The most common cause is **ischaemia** – often multifactorial (technical + patient factors). Never assume a single cause; think of the anastomotic “triangle” – perfusion, tension, and healing environment.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**

There is no universal diagnostic score for anastomotic leak, but the **Dutch Leak Score** (for colorectal leak) uses four variables: fever >38°C (2 points), tachycardia >100 bpm (2), CRP >140 mg/L on day 4-5 (2), and clinical suspicion (1). A score ≥4 has 90% sensitivity for leak. Not mandatory, but useful for threshold thinking.

**Targeted investigations:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Full blood count** | White cell count (WCC), neutrophils | Marker of infection/inflammation | WCC >12 or <4 (left shift), rising trend. But up to 30% of leaks have normal WCC initially. |
| **CRP** | Acute phase reactant | Most useful serial biomarker | CRP >140 mg/L on post-op day 4-5 is a strong predictor. Rising CRP after initial drop is a leak until proven otherwise. |
| **Lactate** | Tissue hypoperfusion / anaerobic metabolism | Identify shock early | Lactate >2 mmol/L in a post-op patient is alarming. >4 is critical. Interpret in context of fluid status and pressors. |
| **Arterial blood gas** | pH, base deficit, lactate | Assess metabolic acidosis | Base deficit < -4 or lactate >2 suggests significant tissue ischaemia or infection. |
| **CT with IV + oral contrast** | Perianastomotic fluid, extraluminal air, contrast extravasation | Gold standard imaging | **Extravasation of oral contrast into the peritoneal cavity is diagnostic.** Free air *may* be normal within first 5 days (surgical emphysema), but a new collection of gas/fluid juxtaposed to the anastomosis is highly suspicious. |
| **CT without contrast** | Usually not sufficient | Only if contraindication to IV contrast or suspected free perforation | Can show collections but misses subtle leaks. |
| **Erect chest X-ray** | Free air under diaphragm | Quick bedside test for perforation | Free air may be present post-laparotomy for up to 7 days. New or increasing free air with clinical deterioration = leak. |
| **Drain fluid analysis** | Amylase, bilirubin, culture | If radiological drain in situ | Amylase >500 U/L in an upper GI leak; bilirubin in biliary leak; culture guides antibiotics. |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms / rules out?**

- **Confirms:** CT with oral contrast showing extravasation outside the bowel lumen.
- **Rules out (near 100% negative predictive value):** A technically adequate CT with IV + oral contrast that shows no extraluminal contrast, no perianastomotic collection, and no free air *in a clinically stable patient*. However, if the patient is unstable, negative imaging should not delay surgical exploration.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – resuscitation and recognition:**

- **Stop oral intake (NBM):** Prevent further spillage. Remove nasogastric tube if in situ (for decompression).
- **IV fluids:** Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline – give a 500 ml bolus stat, then reassess. Aim for urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hour.
- **Blood cultures and start broad-spectrum antibiotics:** Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV (given over 30 min) every 8 hours. If penicillin-allergic, meropenem 1 g IV every 8 hours. Cover Gram-negatives and anaerobes.
- **Call surgical registrar / consultant immediately:** This is not a “review in 30 minutes”. The decision to operate or drain needs senior input now.
- **Consider vasopressors if hypotensive despite fluid:** Noradrenaline (norepinephrine) IV infusion, start 0.05 mcg/kg/min and titrate to MAP >65 mmHg. Do not delay definitive source control for fluid resuscitation.
- **Imaging:** If patient stable enough for CT, go immediately. If unstable, go directly to theatre.

**Common pitfall (first hour):** Do NOT give oral contrast for CT if you suspect a leak – it can be given, but many radiologists prefer water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) which is safer if extravasated. But if the patient is peritonitic, don’t wait for CT – go straight to laparotomy.

**Ward (24-72 hours) – source control and support:**

- **Surgical reintervention:** Either laparoscopic or open. Options: repair the leak (rarely possible), resect and re-anastomose (if healthy tissue), or most commonly: **de-function** – take down the anastomosis, create an end stoma (colostomy/ileostomy), and wash out the abdomen.
- **If contained leak with drainable collection:** CT-guided percutaneous drainage may be attempted if the patient is stable, no generalised peritonitis, and the collection is well-defined. Leave a drain.
- **Continue IV antibiotics:** Duration depends on source control – usually 5-7 days if controlled, longer if ongoing sepsis.
- **Nutritional support:** NBM for at least 5-7 days. TPN (total parenteral nutrition) if prolonged NBM (>7 days) or if the patient is malnourished. Start early (within 48 hours) if pre-existing malnutrition.
- **Monitor:** Daily CRP, WCC, lactate, urine output, fluid balance. Trending CRP downward is a good sign; rising or plateauing suggests ongoing infection.

**Common pitfall (ward phase):** Do NOT restart oral feeds “to see if the leak seals itself” – this will worsen contamination. Do NOT use oral antibiotics in a patient with a leak – they won’t reach the peritoneal cavity effectively.

**Long-term – reconstruction and follow-up:**

- **If a stoma was formed:** Plan for reversal in 3-6 months after resolution of sepsis, adequate nutrition, and treatment of any underlying cause (e.g., smoking cessation, diabetes control). Repeat imaging (contrast enema or colonoscopy) to confirm the defunctioned segment has healed.
- **Enterocutaneous fistula:** May develop if the leak tracks to the skin. Manage with NBM, TPN, octreotide (50-100 mcg SC TID) to reduce GI secretions, and negative pressure wound therapy. Surgical closure after 6-12 months when inflammation has resolved.
- **Oncological implications:** Anastomotic leak after cancer surgery is associated with higher local recurrence risk. These patients need closer surveillance with CEA and colonoscopy.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Anastomotic Leak | Pancreatitis (post-op) | Wound Infection | Ileus (benign) |
|---------|-----------------|------------------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Onset** | Day 5-7 | Day 1-3 | Day 4-7 | Day 1-4 |
| **Pain** | Diffuse, then localised to peritoneum | Epigastric, radiating to back | Localised to wound site | Crampy, poorly localised |
| **Exam** | Guarding, rebound, wound may be clean | Epigastric tenderness, sometimes Cullen’s/Grey-Turner’s | Erythema, warmth, purulent discharge | Mild distension, no peritonism |
| **Vital signs** | Tachycardia, fever, later hypotension | Tachycardia, fever, hypotension can occur | Low-grade fever, normal HR | Usually normal (mild tachycardia if hypovolaemic) |
| **Labs** | Rising CRP, WCC, lactate, base deficit | Elevated amylase/lipase (>3x normal), CRP high | WCC high, CRP moderate, lactate normal | Normal or mildly elevated WCC |
| **Imaging** | CT: extraluminal contrast, air/fluid collection near anastomosis | CT: pancreatic oedema, necrosis, peripancreatic stranding | US/CT: subcutaneous collection, no intra-abdominal fluid | CT: dilated bowel loops, no free fluid, no collection |
| **Key differentiator** | Oral contrast extravasation on CT | Amylase/lipase >3x normal | Wound purulence, normal peritoneum | Clinical improvement with bowel rest and fluids |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Generalised peritonitis** | Severe abdominal pain, board-like rigidity, hypotension, fever | Absent bowel sounds, guarding, rebound, septic shock | Emergency laparotomy, source control, IV antibiotics, ICU admission |
| **Intra-abdominal abscess** | Persistent fever, vague pain, rising CRP/neutrophilia, no bowel function | May have localised tenderness on US/CT | CT-guided percutaneous drainage (if safe) or surgical drainage. Culture-directed antibiotics. |
| **Enterocutaneous fistula** | Drain or wound discharges feculent fluid; patient may develop skin excoriation | High-output fistula >500 ml/day leads to dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, malnutrition | NBM, TPN, octreotide, skin protection (stoma bag), delayed surgical closure (6-12 months) |
| **Septic shock / multi-organ failure** | Hypotension despite fluids, rising lactate, reduced GCS, rising creatinine, need for vasopressors | Lactate >4, base deficit < -8, oliguria, acute kidney injury, respiratory failure | ICU admission, central line, invasive monitoring, vasopressors (noradrenaline), definitive source control. Mortality >30%. |
| **Late presentation – chronic leak / microleak** | Persistent low-grade inflammation, poor wound healing, recurrent ileus, eventual abscess | CRP never normalises, patient fails to progress, may have intermittent fevers | CT with contrast, consider gastrografin enema (for colorectal) to identify occult leak. May require elective repair. |

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic – “LEAKs” for early suspicion:**

- **L** – Look at the **lactate** (any rise >2 is suspicious)
- **E** – **Eyes** – does the patient look unwell (“not right”)?
- **A** – **Abdominal exam** – do not skip a thorough palpation for guarding
- **K** – **KUB** (or CT) – get imaging early
- **S** – **Sepsis screen** – blood cultures, CRP, WCC, lactate

**Mnemonic – “THINK LEAK” for triggers:**

- **T** – Tachycardia (unexplained)
- **H** – Hypotension (new or worsening)
- **I** – Ileus (persistent >5 days)
- **N** – Nutrition (poor pre-op, low albumin)
- **K** – Kind of surgery (low anterior resection, oesophageal)
- **L** – Leucocytosis / raised CRP
- **E** – Electrolyte disturbances
- **A** – Acidosis (metabolic)
- **K** – Keep an open mind – don’t attribute everything to “normal post-op course”

**5-7 high-yield clinical pearls (pattern recognition cues):**

1. **Day 5-7 is the danger zone.** Any unexplained tachycardia, even without fever, on day 4-6 after GI surgery is an anastomotic leak until proven otherwise.
2. **CRP >140 mg/L on day 4 is more sensitive than any clinical sign alone.** Use it as your “rule-out” test – if CRP <120 on day 4, leak is very unlikely.
3. **Pain is not always present.** Elderly, diabetic, or steroid-dependent patients may have silent leaks – look for confusion, oliguria, and rising lactate.
4. **The wound does not have to look infected.** Many leaks occur with a clean, dry wound. Do not be falsely reassured.
5. **Lactate >2 is a red flag** – don’t dismiss as “poor venous access” or “tourniquet”. Repeat arterial gas stat.
6. **Never give oral antibiotics to a patient you suspect of having a leak.** They will not reach the peritoneal cavity and may worsen the contamination.
7. **If you are unsure whether to call the consultant, call.** It is better to be wrong about a leak than to be late.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Attributing tachycardia to pain (give analgesia, recheck – if still tachy, think leak).
- Ordering a “routine” set of bloods and waiting for results before calling a senior.
- Forgetting to assess urine output – it’s the only objective measure of volume status.
- Misinterpreting free air on CT as post-operative – any new or increasing free air beyond day 4 is pathological.
- Starting oral feeds “to stimulate the bowel” – can turn a contained leak into a diffuse peritonitis.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call for help – thresholds to act:**

- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (especially with respiratory rate >20, oxygen saturation <94%, or systolic BP <100 mmHg) – call surgical registrar immediately.
- **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** on any arterial sample – escalate, do not wait for repeat.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** – this is a **critical** finding – call consultant and consider ICU.
- **GCS < 15 or acute confusion** – possible sepsis-related encephalopathy – call ICU outreach.
- **Peritonitis on examination** (guarding, rigidity, rebound) – immediate surgical review; patient likely needs theatre.
- **Sustained hypotension** (SBP <90 mmHg after 500 ml fluid bolus) – start noradrenaline and call consultant/anaesthetist.
- **Urine output <0.5 ml/kg/hour for >2 hours** – despite fluid challenges – sign of ongoing hypovolaemia or developing acute kidney injury – escalate.
- **Any concern from nursing staff** that the patient looks “unwell” – trust it. Get a senior to review.

**When to call the crash team / ICU:**

- If the patient has a **cardiac or respiratory arrest** – obviously.
- If **SBP <80 mmHg despite 2 L fluid and vasopressors started** – call ICU for central line admission.
- If **GCS deteriorates to <8** – protect airway, call anaesthetics.
- If **lactate >6 mmol/L** despite initial resuscitation – likely need organ support.

**Quote to remember:** “When you think of an anastomotic leak, you are already late. You need to have thought of it yesterday.” Act early, act decisively. Your patient’s survival depends on it.
